summary:
The Construction of Versailles (1661–1710 AD) was the greatest architectural and political project of 17th-century Europe — [[event:2361|Louis XIV's]] transformation of his father's modest hunting lodge at Versailles into the grandest palace in the world, simultaneously housing the French court (and thus controlling the nobility), projecting French cultural supremacy, and embodying the absolutist ideology of the Sun King in physical form. The project employed over 36,000 workers at its peak, consumed an enormous portion of France's royal revenues, and required the draining of swamps and relocation of entire villages. The palace's Hall of Mirrors (Galerie des Glaces), the formal gardens designed by André Le Nôtre, the Grand Trianon, and the endless state apartments created an environment of overwhelming luxury and symbolic meaning — every detail expressed royal power.

Versailles served multiple political functions beyond displaying magnificence. By requiring nobles to spend time at court rather than on their provincial estates, Louis transformed potentially dangerous feudal lords into courtiers dependent on royal favor for advancement — domesticating the aristocracy that had rebelled in the Fronde (1648–1653). The rigid etiquette of Versailles, in which every moment of the king's day was ritualized and attendance at ceremonies was both mandatory and politically meaningful, created a social system in which proximity to the king was the highest status. France under Louis became the cultural model for all of Europe: French language, fashion, architecture, cuisine, and court etiquette were imitated from Madrid to Moscow, establishing a French cultural dominance that would persist until the 20th century. Versailles remained the seat of French royal government until [[event:2353|the Revolution]] forced the royal family back to Paris in October 1789.
